sherlock-project MCP server sherlock username search
sherlock-project sherlock CLI tool API key authentication required
Sherlock project MCP server official model context protocol

researched_category.notes: Sherlock is an open-source intelligence (OSINT) tool for finding usernames across social networks.

one_liner.value: Hunt down social media accounts by username across 400+ social networks
one_liner.notes: This is the tagline from the official GitHub repository and project website.

methods.notes: Sherlock is a CLI/library tool that does NOT require any API keys or login credentials. It works by constructing profile URLs and observing HTTP responses. No authentication is needed to use the core tool.

authentication.notes: The Sherlock project has no web service that requires authentication. It is a self-hosted CLI tool and Python library. An experimental self-hosted REST API exists (sherlock-project/api) but has no auth mechanism documented.

access.notes: Sherlock is MIT-licensed open source. You can install it via pip/pipx/Docker immediately with no account, payment, or approval needed. The only 'gate' is having Python installed.

summary.value: Sherlock is primarily a CLI tool and Python library. There is no official public REST/GraphQL API. An experimental, unmaintained Django REST Framework API exists at github.com/sherlock-project/api (last commit ~2021) but requires local hosting. The primary interface is the `sherlock` command-line tool or importing `sherlock_project` as a Python module. There is also a community-maintained MCP server that wraps the CLI.
summary.notes: The core project is a CLI/library, not a web API service. The experimental API project has 82 stars, is unmaintained, and requires self-hosting.

agent_interface.notes: A community MCP server (Burnsedia/sherlock-mcp) exists that wraps Sherlock as a FastMCP server with a search_username tool. It requires the Sherlock CLI to be pre-installed. There is no official MCP server from the Sherlock Project.

rationale.value: Sherlock can be built into an AI agent toolkit today. It is an open-source (MIT) Python library/CLI tool with no authentication gates, no API keys required, and no paid plans. A community MCP server already exists demonstrating this. An agent can either call the `sherlock` CLI command or import the `sherlock_project` Python package directly. The only consideration is that it's a locally-run tool (not a cloud API), so the agent/host running it must have Sherlock installed.
rationale.notes: The main blocker is non-material. The tool is fully self-serve and open source. The lack of an official cloud-hosted API means the agent must have execution environment access to run the CLI or Python package, but this is typical for many agent toolkits.

s1.quote: Hunt down social media accounts by username across social networks
s1.title: sherlock-project/sherlock

s2.quote: Sherlock is designed to be straightforward for open source investigations: it does not require API keys or login credentials for the sites it checks; instead, it simply constructs the expected profile URL for each site and observes the response to determine whether the username exists on a given platform.
s2.title: Sherlock | Bellingcat's Online Investigation Toolkit

s4.quote: FastMCP server integration for the Sherlock OSINT tool – Seamlessly search social media accounts across 400+ platforms using the Model Context Protocol.
s4.title: Burnsedia/sherlock-mcp

s6.quote: Hunt down social media accounts by username across 400+ social networks
s6.title: Sherlock Project

s7.quote: Hunt down social media accounts by username across social networks
s7.title: sherlock-project/sherlock

limitations: The authentication model is 'custom' because Sherlock is not a web service - it's a locally-run CLI tool with no auth mechanism at all. This is inherent to its design as a self-hosted OSINT tool.
The experimental REST API (sherlock-project/api) is unmaintained (last commit ~2021) and has no documented authentication; it was not factored into the primary analysis.
Some third-party services (e.g., Apify) wrap Sherlock as a cloud API with API-key auth, but these are separate platforms, not the core Sherlock project.
